query: Why are props immutable in React interview question

competitors.label: Components and Hooks must be pure
competitors.selectionReason: This is the official React rules page that explicitly states props and state are immutable snapshots.
competitors.winReason: The official rule page is authoritative, but our page is more explicit about production debugging fallout: broken memoization, hidden side effects, and in-place array mutation corrupting sibling views.
theirStrengths: It is the canonical source for React purity rules and immutable snapshots.
theirGaps: It is rule-oriented and lighter on production incident framing.

realWorldUseCases: If a child mutates those inputs, rendering stops being a pure computation and you get hidden side effects, broken memoization assumptions, and shared mutable state bugs that are painful to debug in production.
followUpConfusion: Sorting, pushing, splicing, or reversing a prop array mutates in-place and can corrupt parent/sibling views.

realWorldUseCases: The rule page explains that props are immutable snapshots, but it is less bug-first about parent-child contamination and array mutation fallout.
actionableExamples: It includes mutation-versus-copy guidance, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer about why React still behaves as if props are immutable even though JavaScript itself allows mutation.

actionableExamples: Add one short fixed-code example that replaces in-place array mutation with a copied update.

competitors.label: Basic React Concepts for Interviews
competitors.selectionReason: This playbook page is the strongest GreatFrontEnd baseline because it explicitly covers props versus state and says props are immutable.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd states the rule clearly, but our answer goes further into why prop mutation breaks purity, memoization assumptions, and shared array/object identity in production code.
theirStrengths: It is concise and directly aligned with interview prep on props versus state.
theirGaps: It states that props are immutable but does not dwell on mutation bugs and timing-sensitive fallout.

realWorldUseCases: Props are inputs owned by the parent.
followUpConfusion: If you mutate props, you introduce hidden side effects: missed updates, cross-component contamination, and timing-sensitive bugs.

realWorldUseCases: The playbook identifies props as immutable inputs from the parent, but it is more definitional than incident-driven.
actionableExamples: It includes direct props and state examples, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is stronger on the follow-up around mutated arrays/objects and why memoization can silently break.

actionableExamples: Add one worked example of a child sorting a prop array in place and contaminating a sibling view.

competitors.label: Prop Immutability
competitors.selectionReason: This rule page is a strong tutorial/reference competitor because it focuses exactly on why mutating props is an anti-pattern.
theirStrengths: It is tightly scoped to prop immutability and includes strong broken-versus-fixed examples.
theirGaps: It is more style-guide oriented than compact interview delivery.

actionableExamples: Copy nested structures too when a child needs a transformed version

realWorldUseCases: It discusses real bugs from mutating objects and arrays that came from props.
actionableExamples: It includes more explicit before-and-after code examples than our current answer.
followUpConfusion: Both pages explain parent ownership and why child mutation violates React's model, so this criterion stays close.

realWorldUseCases: Add one short scenario where a memoized child stops updating predictably because a prop object was mutated in place.
actionableExamples: Add a copy-based fix example for arrays and nested objects.
followUpConfusion: Keep one extra short example showing when local draft state is allowed even though props remain read-only.

competitors.winReason: Our page now adds a memoized-child mutation bug and a nested copy-based fix, so it is stronger on production use-cases and actionable examples while the broad conceptual explanation stays close.
